Title: Biosynthesis of L-ascorbic acid in plants: new pathways for an old antioxidant

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant; redox homeostasis; development and stress responses
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Transcripts decrease after exogenous L-ascorbic acid and in non-irradiated tobacco leaf halves
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcripts decrease after exogenous L-ascorbic acid and in non-irradiated tobacco leaf halves
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcript analysis in tobacco suspension cells and leaves
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with transcriptional feedback and light-responsive regulation of L-ascorbic acid biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Tobacco GDP-mannose pyrophosphorylase transcripts decrease after exogenous ascorbate addition. 2. Its transcripts also decrease in non-irradiated leaf halves. 3. These observations support transcriptional regulation of vitamin C biosynthesis by feedback and light.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review specifically discusses its transcriptional regulation in relation to ascorbate biosynthesis, but direct causal validation as a focal core enzyme is limited in this paper.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential upstream engineering target, though regulatory significance here is based mainly on transcript correlations

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Lactuca sativa; Nicotiana tabacum; Arabidopsis thaliana
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant; redox homeostasis; development and stress responses
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression in lettuce and tobacco; ectopic expression rescue in Arabidopsis vtc mutants; metabolite assays
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases L-ascorbic acid up to sevenfold and rescues low-ascorbate Arabidopsis vtc mutants
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of the rat L-gulono-1,4-lactone oxidase in lettuce and tobacco strongly increases L-ascorbic acid. 2. Ectopic expression rescues leaf ascorbate content in Arabidopsis vtc mutants. 3. These data suggest plants can support an animal-like terminal step or provide the corresponding substrate, although this does not prove operation in wild-type plants.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a native plant gene; cited as evidence for possible alternative pathway capacity rather than definitive endogenous plant pathway activity.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible introduction of non-native pathway activity complicates interpretation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful proof-of-concept transgene for vitamin C biofortification, but non-native origin and pathway ambiguity are major cautions

Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; measurement of reduced:oxidized ratio and total L-ascorbic acid
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases reduced:oxidized ascorbate ratio and raises total L-ascorbic acid content by 2–4 fold through enhanced recycling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Increasing dehydroascorbate reductase expression boosts the reduced:oxidized ascorbate ratio. 2. Overexpression of a wheat dehydroascorbate reductase in tobacco and maize increases total ascorbate 2–4 fold. 3. Enhanced recycling likely prevents irreversible dehydroascorbate breakdown, thereby elevating net vitamin C levels.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts in recycling rather than de novo biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong engineering target for boosting vitamin C via recycling, potentially complementary to biosynthetic gene stacking

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream branch point preceding committed step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Epimerizes GDP-D-mannose at the 3′ and/or 5′ positions to produce GDP-L-galactose and GDP-L-gulose
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms immediate precursor pool and creates alternative branch toward L-ascorbic acid
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant; contributes to cellular redox homeostasis; modulates plant development through hormone signalling; supports stress responses
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Chloroplast stroma and other plant tissues
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Stress; cellular redox state; light discussed for pathway regulation generally
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant and native enzyme assays; product identification; in vitro inhibition assays
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as an important regulatory step and key control point; precedes the committed irreversible hydrolysis step
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes L-ascorbic acid biosynthesis by supplying GDP-L-galactose and GDP-L-gulose; enzyme activity is feedback-inhibited by L-ascorbic acid and L-galactono-1,4-lactone
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis GDP-mannose 3′,5′-epimerase converts GDP-D-mannose to both GDP-L-galactose and GDP-L-gulose. 2. Discovery of GDP-L-gulose established a new branch linking plant and animal-like L-ascorbic acid pathways. 3. The enzyme is inhibited by L-ascorbic acid and L-galactono-1,4-lactone and responds to the cellular redox state, supporting a regulatory role in vitamin C biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review article summarizing prior studies. Oxidized nicotinamide nucleotides stimulate activity, whereas NADH/NADPH inhibit it; molecular form may influence product distribution.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising metabolic engineering target to tune vitamin C levels, but branch usage and regulation are context dependent and require validation under specific tissues/conditions

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant; redox regulator; developmental and stress-related functions
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Chloroplast stroma and other plant tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expression correlates with diurnal changes of L-ascorbic acid in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression correlates with diurnal changes of L-ascorbic acid in Arabidopsis; transcripts decrease in non-irradiated tobacco leaf halves and after exogenous ascorbate in tobacco suspension cells
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light/non-irradiation; exogenous L-ascorbic acid; mitochondrial electron flow state
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme assays in plant mitochondria; transcriptional analyses; physical association study with mitochondrial complex I
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as an important regulatory step; optimal flux requires mitochondrial complex I electron flow
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly produces L-ascorbic acid; its activity/expression is associated with vitamin C accumulation and redox-linked mitochondrial function
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GLDH catalyzes the last step of the plant-specific L-ascorbic acid pathway. 2. The enzyme is an integral inner mitochondrial membrane protein physically linked to mitochondrial complex I, and complex I electron flow is required for optimal biosynthesis. 3. GLDH transcript abundance is reduced by exogenous ascorbate and darkness/non-irradiation and correlates with diurnal vitamin C changes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review article summarizing prior studies in several species including pea, mung bean, tobacco and Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for increasing vitamin C, but mitochondrial respiratory context and feedback regulation should be considered

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces pectin-derived D-galacturonic acid to L-galactonic acid, which can spontaneously lactonize to L-galactono-1,4-lactone
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies immediate precursor of the terminal GLDH step
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant; redox regulator; developmental and stress-related functions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning and characterization; precursor feeding/radiotracer context; enzyme activity detection
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports an alternative uronic acid pathway contributing to L-ascorbic acid biosynthesis in ripening strawberry fruit
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A D-galacturonic acid reductase was cloned and characterized from strawberry fruit. 2. The enzyme supports the proposed uronic acid pathway by reducing pectin-derived D-galacturonic acid to L-galactonic acid. 3. This branch may be especially relevant in ripening/senescing fruit tissues where pectin turnover supplies substrate.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: L-galactonic acid is proposed to spontaneously convert to L-galactono-1,4-lactone.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux likely depends on substrate availability from cell wall turnover
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential fruit-specific target to elevate vitamin C during ripening

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes myo-inositol to D-glucuronic acid, a proposed precursor for L-ascorbic acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies precursor pool for an alternative vitamin C pathway
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant; redox regulator; developmental and stress-related functions
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Flowers and leaves discussed as primary expression sites
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Primarily expressed in flowers and leaves of Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Primarily expressed in flowers and leaves of Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequence-based homology search in Arabidopsis identified four ORFs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Homology search; cloning; bacterial recombinant enzyme assay; ectopic expression in Arabidopsis; metabolite measurement
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Ectopic expression significantly increases L-ascorbic acid levels, supporting a possible alternative precursor route from myo-inositol
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis contains a small myo-inositol oxygenase gene family, and one cloned coding region encodes active enzyme. 2. Recombinant protein converts myo-inositol to D-glucuronic acid. 3. Ectopic expression significantly increases L-ascorbic acid levels in Arabidopsis, although in vivo contribution of this route remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tracer studies in detached ripening strawberry fruit and parsley leaves argued against myo-inositol as a major precursor in those tissues; pathway contribution is tissue dependent and uncertain.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: D-glucuronic acid can also feed cell wall polymer synthesis, potentially competing with vitamin C biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering vitamin C, but redundancy and diversion into cell wall metabolism complicate deployment
